text: The surgeon uses an 8mm robotic trocar in the left upper quadrant to develop the retroactive space, followed by blunt dissection with the camera.
The surgeon places the middle port after creating sufficient space in the surgical field, using robotic assistance.
The surgeon uses a finder needle to guide the placement of the port near the semilunar line in the left lower quadrant.
The surgeon begins to fully develop the ipsilateral retrorectus space, completing the dissection in preparation for initiating a crossover technique.
The surgeon examines the lower abdomen, focusing below the arcuate line in the space of Retzius, where a limited crossover has been initiated.
The surgeon returns to the upper abdomen to perform a crossover dissection above the falciform ligament, using visual guidance to navigate the anatomical structures.
The surgeon carefully leaves a small lip of fascia on the ipsilateral side, ensuring it remains visible for subsequent closure.
The surgeon reduces the peritoneum, carefully avoiding the creation of holes, although two small holes are visible in the peritoneal layer.
The surgeon continues to open the posterior sheath on the ipsilateral side, extending the dissection towards the lower abdomen.
The surgeon works below the arcuate line, progressing towards the space of Retzius, where a limited crossover has already been performed.
The surgeon identifies the arcuate line on the right side of the patient and employs a bottoms-up approach to open the contralateral posterior sheath, revealing the right retro-rectus space.
The surgeon adjusts the angle of approach with the right arm to reduce more of the hernia and the adjacent peritoneum visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon fully reduces the hernia over to the contralateral right rectus, then completes the opening of the posterior rectus sheath on the right side of the patient to further develop the right retro-rectus space.
The surgeon creates additional space in the upper abdomen and initiates entry into the retro-rectus space on the right-hand side, focusing on the high upper abdominal area.
The surgeon assesses the preperitoneal plane and determines that further dissection lateral to the current area is unnecessary, indicating a stable surgical field.
The surgeon carefully uses electrocautery on the anterior pseudosac while avoiding the posterior peritoneum, occasionally switching to cold scissors for precise dissection.
The surgeon reaches the lateral side of the hernia and drops the posterior rectus sheath, opting not to continue dissection in the preperitoneal plane.

The surgeon is positioned on the lateral side of the hernia defect at the junction of the medial edge of the right rectus muscle. The posterior rectus sheath is being carefully dissected and taken down, allowing access to the entire right retro-rectus space. The dissection is extended out to the semilunar line.
The surgeon creates additional space in the upper abdomen to ensure sufficient mesh overlap above the hernia defects.
The surgeon proceeds to close the posterior holes in the surgical field, ensuring the integrity of the posterior layer.
The surgeon visually estimates the size of the hernia defect using surgical instruments, then begins closing the defect starting from the top.

The surgeon continues to preserve the lip of fascia on the ipsilateral side, providing a visible structure to sew to, especially in a patient with a narrow rectus muscle, while maintaining proximity to the camera view.
The surgeon begins suturing from the bottom of the hernia defect, using reduced pressure to facilitate closure of the anterior sheath.
The surgeon imbricates the Pseudosac, folding and suturing it to reduce dead space and minimize the risk of seroma formation.
The surgeon sutures the tissue, selecting stitches based on optimal angle and visibility, and ties them together for added security.
The surgeon measures the space and places a large piece of mesh to fill the area, ensuring it lies flat without folding, bowing, or bunching.
The surgeon measures the space accurately and cuts the mesh to fit the hernia repair site. The mesh is positioned neatly without folding or bunching.
The surgeon places a drain in the surgical site to manage fluid accumulation, ensuring the mesh and surrounding tissues sit flat within the large space created during the procedure.
